content:
# Top 5 posts from r/MovieSuggestions day

## 1. Movies where female lead have some actual goals in life not just rebelling or finding love.

**Score:** 46
**Body:** Like Frances Ha, violet evergarden and most Ghibli movie... gentle, grounded storytelling where a girl isn’t chasing romance or rebelling against her family for drama, but just… living, growing, finding meaning, getting by and working towards her own goals with emotional honesty and beautiful relationship with her family and all. Something slice of life and feel good, focused on quiet growth and independence....not romance obviously.

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. • Girlfriends (1978) - it's similar to Frances Ha


• Flashdance (1983)


• Chicago (2002)


• Mrs Harris Goes To Paris (2022)


• Funny Girl (1968) 


• Little Women (1994)
2. Hidden Figures 

Erin Brockovitch 

Sister Act
3. Career goals and romance:

Save the Last Dance (2001)

Til There Was You (1997) 

Working Girl (1988)

Pillow Talk (1959)
  - Came here for working girl.
    - Yup me too. *Working Girl* was the film that made every woman realize being a secretary was mostly a dead-end position with no pay raises. So upper management renamed secretary to administrative assistant.
4. You may have to go back a bit.

1928	The Garden of Eden

1931	Mädchen in Uniform

1932	Frisco Jenny

1932	The Red-Headed Woman

1933	Baby Face

1933	Torch Singer

1933	Victor and Victoria

1934	The Scarlet Empress

1936	Libeled Lady

1937	One Hundred Men and a Girl

1950	Annie Get Your Gun

1987	Bagdad Cafe

1991	Mrs. Warren's Profession
5. Mona Lisa Smile. 


Romance figures in the movie but the overarching theme is women finding meaning in their lives given the constraints and ideals of the time. 


It might not precisely fit your requirements but it's an amazing movie. 
6. Given how difficult it is to find movies with female leads who have actual life goals, you might want to look up the Bechdel Movie Test. It was written to show how most movies are centered on men and their experiences. How there aren't many honest depictions of women and their experiences, dreams and actions.

Movies that pass the Bechdel Test films have at minimum:

1) at least two women in it, who (2) who talk to each other, about (3) something other than the men. 

It's astonishing and sad that it's hard to find films that pass the test even with such a low bar. And more sad and infuriating because even if the movie passes the test, it doesn't mean that it isn't sexist or focused on men.
7. Devil wears prada
8. To Die For!

## 2. Any horror movies were the characters aren't incompetent?

**Score:** 26
**Body:** I want to watch a horror movie where the characters fight back, and not like in a super geared up kind of way where they're prepared from the get go. I want to watch something where a character defends themself in a feasible way. Movies I've watched the come to mind are Cabin in the Woods and Weapons. Like for example when Julia Garners character faced off against her ex-lover ( I think?) and didn't hesitate to fight back, it was like a realistic survival instinct.
The movie itself doesn't have to be realistic. Thanks in advance for your suggestions :) Bonus points if there is no standard horror movie type music!

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Ready or Not
2. Event Horizon.

The characters were an elite, deep space search and rescue team babysitting a scientist who developed a form of faster than light travel. They were masters of their jobs, but none of them were prepared for supernatural horrors.
3. [You’re Next](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/You%27re_Next)
4. If memory serves, Anya Taylor Joy's character in The Menu was pretty savvy and proactive. The film is a horror satire and I found it super fun.
  - Huge fan of her's. I've been putting this movie off for no good reason. Will watch soon. Thank you!
5. Gotta go with a classic, The Thing (1980). A ahole group of smart scientests is a giod definition of not incompetent characters :)
  - How did I forget to mention I've watched The Thing!!! I am a fan of the movie! Haha thanks!
6. The Hunt cones to mind, but it’s not exactly horror.
7. Happy Death Day :)
8. Just about the whole Alien franchise.

## 3. I want a movie like Gone Girl, in the sense that it is a mystery pieced together.

**Score:** 22
**Body:** I would like to watch a film like Gone Girl, that becomes pieced together, piece by piece, with twists that shock you. Any murder mystery recs, or missing people recs are also welcomed. Or TV series, just something as good, with a slightly better ending.

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Rear Window is a fun one!
2. Knives Out
3. Memento
4. Frailty (2001)

The Gift (2015)

Primal Fear (1996)
5. Disappearance at Clifton Hill

Prisoners

Nocturnal Animals
6. Not a film but a limited series, Sharp Objects by the same writer Gillian Flynn.
7. The kid detective was a great movie I didn’t see make too many headlines.
8. Memento

Tv: Dept Q, The Sinner, A Murder at the End of the World
9. Mystic River, with Sean Penn
10. The girl with the dragon tattoo

## 4. Please Recommend Movies That Feel Like a Warm Hug (Similar to Good Will Hunting)

**Score:** 19
**Body:** movies that help a character, movies where a troubled character grows out of it with love and mentorship - something like Good Will Hunting (my GOAT). God I miss Robin Williams so much. His performance in it felt just so real.

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. The Holdovers
2. Billy Elliot

Harold And Maude

Young Hearts

Dead Poets Society

Honey Boy

Marcel The Shell

About Time
3. Chef (2014) is my go-to movie to show people when I just want something nice and lovely. A character is a bit troubled and does move himself along. He mentors his son. So it’s a bit different than your formula but still charming.
4. Finding Forrester (2000)

Dead Poets Society (1989)
5. The Station Agent (2003)
6. Strictly Ballroom
7. Billy Elliot
8. Scent of a women (1992) 

I absolutely love that film
9. Paddinton 2
10. Oddball

## 5. Dystopian Sci-Fi Movie Recommendations?

**Score:** 15
**Body:** Hey all!

I’m looking for some dystopian science fiction movie recommendations.

Such as:   
* The Matrix.  
* Gattaca.   
* Dark City.  
* Children of Men.  
* Equilibrium.

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Blade Runner

1984

Brazil

12 Monkeys

Code 46

Never Let Me Go
2. District 9 if you haven’t seen it
3. Soylent Green
4. Akira
5. Aniara
6. V for Vendetta

Running Man

Alita: Battle Angel

1984

Hunger Games & sequels
7. La Jetee
8. Vesper
9. A Boy and His Dog 

Soylent Green 

Zardoz
10. Brazil


1984


Logan's Run

# Top 5 posts from r/moviecritic day

## 1. Best movie set on a ship?

**Score:** 1943

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Das boot
  - DIRECTORS CUT (1991)
2. I'd go with Master and Commander TOOO. The scene where the crew plays music on deck after a brutal storm always gets me and Russell Crowe’s performance , no words are needed.
  - "the wreckage is acting as a sea anchor, we must cut it loose!"

*Mr. Allen looking at Captain Jack with a sad, solemn face waiting for the order.* 

*Jack nods*
    - Jack Aubrey’s intro is brilliant too. 

The poor midshipman (?) who’s the lookout on deck spots what he thinks is sails far off in the distance, sounds the alarm and musters the ship including the captain who gets out of bed and properly dressed up, but he doesn’t yet see anything with his monocular, and the midshipman feels bad that he made a big fuss for apparently nothing. 

Jack says think nothing of it, much better safe than sorry etc, but then beautifully he doesn’t just go back to bed, he’s like wait a minute - I trust these guys. Maybe he really saw something. So he goes back to double check and sure enough. 

That instantly tells you everything you need to know about the man and how he runs his ship. 
3. Jaws
4. Alien. Not sure if it counts but technically a ship
5. The Hunt for Red October
  - I would like to have seen Montana…
6. I would go with Mutiny on the bounty 1962

## 2. What are some of the most iconic debut performances ever?

**Score:** 1627
**Body:** In frame: Edward Norton in Primal Fear. 

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Tim Curry 

The Rocky Horror Picture Show (1975)

“…iconic…”
  - Woah THAT was his first movie??
2. I’d also pick Alan Rickman as Hans Gruber in Die Hard. He was already a phenomenal stage actor by that time but considering it was his film debut, it’d count I guess.
3. Primal Fear is still one of my favorite movies. Edward is phenomenal! Whole cast is great. Love it!
4. Sigourney weaver - alien
5. Obligatory -Christoph Waltz for his U.S. debut in Inglorious Basterds
  - That’s a bingo!
6. I don't know that anyone can match Val Kilmer's start.   
Top Secret! - Real Genius - Top Gun - Willow

For a specific debut though, I agree with Dafne Keen in Logan.  She was amazing.
7. Haillee Steinfeld in True Grit
8. Julie Andrews in Mary Poppins.

## 3. Ace Ventura at the Cannibal Corpse concert is peak 90's comedy.

**Score:** 262

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Excuse me is Greg here??!!
  - Thank you…
  - We have a friend Greg who bartends at one of our favorite places. We ask this when we go in sometimes. We’re not sure if everyone gets the reference 🤣
2. I can hear this gif
3. New England Clam Chowder
  - Is that the red or the white?
4. I watched for 30 years before I knew this scene existed. This scene and the Pig Skin bar scene. I guess they were only included on the TBS version.
5. Jim Carrey is a huge fan of CC and insisted on them being the band for this shot https://www.reddit.com/r/Metal/s/7IUsJfoIK9
  - Beat me to saying it. Like he knew their actual lyrics and shit. And this was before people outside of the FL Metal scene really knew CC that well, so its impressive to me.
6. "Therrrres something in the pit. Some... Thing"

## 4. I loved Prometheus

**Score:** 263
**Body:** Knowing that this movie divided a lot of people including, critics, general audiences, fans of the franchise and sci-fi movies, I wasn't sure what to expect from this movie coming out as a new Alien fan.

I loved it! That said, I can't explain about why without acting like a pretentious snob, >!because I'm afraid of what people might think of me for liking its elements unusual of me to like... but such illogical moments such as the scientists touching that alien snake, wanting to see that engineer alien guy get killed by that giant facehugger due to me wanting to know about the dangers of the planet, it's dreadful yet interesting to watch. And the most weird thing about me liking about this movie is that it makes me want more of Alien due to the unanswered questions, this reason which I can understand why people are divided of this movie, but I can't help but sense it's what made the Alien movie great, but then again, it answered the questions about where the Xenomorphs come from... The fear of the unknown by not answering any questions which is why Alien would also work as a movie itself, and I don't think I want any of its questions answered... It actually makes its universe feel more grand and big...!< 

In a non-pretentious answer, I was invested in this movie in reasons I can't say, I honestly like it if the movie left me confused. It's beautiful and horrifying in some scenes. I can't say if I liked it both either as an Alien installment or as a movie on its own. I understand why it divided everyone but I never expect to be part of its fans!

Any thoughts of this movie?

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. That a nice perspective - the fear of the unknown and not answering all the questions.

It’s a stunning film and has so many epic moments. You could look past the plot holes if it didn’t have those dumb moments you mentioned. They really do make for eye rolls and wtf.

This was an interesting watch recently that highlighted a few potential back story things I didn’t know about. Quite interesting

[https://youtu.be/BF_x3QueXSw?si=QS-JH9dq1dbmqkAA](https://youtu.be/BF_x3QueXSw?si=QS-JH9dq1dbmqkAA)
2. I love all the Alien movies for a variety of reasons (I'm not much for the Predator crossovers though). I think each movie brings something unique to the table be it horror, action, aesthetics, concepts, etc. Are any of them perfect? No. But few movies are. But I still love them.
  - True! Ripley carried the entire franchise on her shoulders — and half the xenomorph population on her back.
3. Prometheus has weathered the storm of its own mistakes. Yes there were dumb decisions in the plot, but the world building and a mystery being solved was compelling.
4. I personally enjoied Romulus aswell.
5. Saw it in the theater with my father and was thrilled... Thinking back, I was already intrigued by the whole Ancient Alien thing (was a hit show on the History channel at the time) and so the only thing I really wanted was more of that "intellectual" discussion of the psuedo-science. Tying into the already established ALIEN lore. 

The thing about esoteria (as opposed to blatant exposition) is that it WORKS as entertainment. I'm a big fan of Elden Ring and the whole Dark Souls approach to video gaming, for instance.
6. I’ve enjoyed every single alien film.

While some are not classics they are all enjoyable.
7. You like the movie. Good for you.

I did not like the move. I did not like the characters. I did not like the plot.
8. I still occasionally watch it on Disney+. 

There is a sense of grandeur with that film, the music, the visuals, and even the story of seeking out their creator. I will say, though, it was a shame they didn't pursue this story any further and were aiming to tie it all back to the Alien franchise. I would have liked the second film (Alien Covenant) to focus more on Elizabeth's journey to the world of the Engineers. I would prefer if they explored that story more than David's obsession with creating the 'perfect' specimen. 

Seems like they just retconned that and brought back the 'Alien' elements instead.
9. The film's big ideas >> smaller logic issues. 

Alien Covenant is also great. It has many of the same strengths and weaknesses of Prometheus, while also being perversely gleeful about dicking the audience around, as dark an ending as any studio film has ever had.

## 5. What’s one song that became so iconic because of the movie it was used in, that you can’t hear it without instantly thinking of that film?

**Score:** 221
**Body:** (Love Me again by John Newman - Still from Edge of Tomorrow)

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Reservoir Dogs.  You know the song and the scene.
  - To this day I still can’t go to a torture party without this song popping into my head.
2. Danger Zone.
  - Well if we’re talking Kenny Loggins we can’t forget Footloose. Iconic.
3. All Star, Shrek
4. Simple Minds Don’t You Forget About Me Breakfast Club.
5. Stuck in the Middle with You- Stealers Wheel, from Reservoir Dogs
6. Eye of the Tiger- Rocky 3!!!
7. I Got You Babe - Groundhog Day.
8. Ride of the Valkyries - Apocalypse Now

---

# Top 5 posts from r/TrueFilm day

## 1. Is Del Toro following Tim Burton?

**Score:** 47
**Body:** I know alot of ppl like Frankenstein, but also seeing a lot of push back - saying that his distinctive style has started to erode into generic hollywood (even though it's on Netflix) + that his recent films have lacked depth. It reminds me of other film makers who've entered a similar stage like Burton who a - struggled to adapt to shooting on digital cameras & CGI and b - Remaking all their dream childhood favourites (like Alice and Dumbo) actually just revealed that their previous pieces, that were simply inspired by those classics - were far more interesting. 

I could go into specifics of why I think Frankenstein didn't work... but there's been a fair few posts already on that, so I'll just do some quick bullet points:

\- Lack of any horror or attemps for scares.

\- Almost Marvel-esque looking action set pieces (or John Wick Monster Fu) 

\- Oddly digital look for period/gothic subject matter - Over lit, low contrast grade mixed with Wide Angle lenses on a very mobile/rigged cameras. 

\- Too pretty and clean make-up'd design for the monster (His only grotesque qualities were short lived cgi wolverine effects).

\- morally scrubbed of nuance.  

\- A far more interesting/subversive take on the story came out v recently - Poor Things.

\- Flat Characters - lack of interesting dialogue (shouldn't write by himself), Mia was the only one giving a compelling performance but her character was very 2025 and far too quick to trust the monster, an attempt to make her less of an object of desire/ damsel in distress over compensates into giving her the personality equivalent of 'iphone face'. 



### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Have you read the book? The original book is hardly scary. I really don't get that criticism at all. Even compared to another older novel like Dracula, that does have some genuine dread and horror elements, it isn't scary at all.
2. > Lack of any horror or attemps for scares

Well because it is not really a horror movie, and the book is not really a horror book, while it has elements of it that was mostly amplified by previous movie adaptations, it is more of a gothic sci-fi drama.  

I didn't love the movie either, I liked it but I wasn't crazy about it, but I don't think that specific point if a fair criticism, because that is more of a fault with your expectations than the movie itself.

> Mia was the only one giving a compelling performance but her character was very 2025 and far too quick to trust the monster

I am not sure what this even means
3. My thoughts on Frankenstein is that it is undoubtedly a Del Toro film. The wardrobe, the set pieces, the music, the camera movement. Just like someone can tell a Wes Anderson film is a Wes Anderson Film, Frankenstein had Del Toro written all over it. This film is almost a companion piece to Crimson Peak and Shape of Water it was so similar. If you blind screened this without revealing the director I'm positive 90% of people could have guessed it. 

And that's not a bad thing. I understand some of the criticisms however l will push back and boldly claim that I don't think anyone can make Frankenstein new or fresh.
4. No. There has never been a more Del Toro movie than Frankenstein, for better and worse.

Additionally, Nightmare Alley is straight fucking fire, possibly my favorite of his movies. Under-appreciated for not being the kind of movie people were looking for at the time, and a break from what he was typically making, but still covered in his fingerprints. A bifurcated narrative will always polarize people. If you were enjoying the movie you were watching and suddenly a new major story thread starts, it's always going to take some people out. It happened to me with Frankenstein, actually. The moment Cate Blanchett shows up in Nightmare Alley though, man, it gets really fucking fun and you see why GDT really wanted to make it.
  - I think Frankenstein and Nightmare Alley suffer from some of the same pacing issues  - GDT has so much fun creating the world of the film and luxuriating in it that it makes the overall result feel somewhat lopsided.  Maybe this is a heretical thought, but both films would flow better if they were under two hours. 

I am a massive fan of the 1947 Nightmare Alley because the script is lean and mean in a way GDT's was not.  His circus world is rich and beautiful and, to me, it felt like he enjoyed that part of the story far more than the rest of the plot.  The production design was stunning though. 

The first part of Frankenstein was so long and drawn out - I didn't love the how long it took to get to the Creature.  Again the production design is stunning but there was almost too much runway to get to the meat of the story.  Elordi was incredible as the Creature and was far and away the best part to me.
    - See I love the first half of Frankenstein and the second half of Nightmare Alley and don't care for the other halves nearly as much. The circus world looks cool but the story really gets cooking when they take the show on the road and meet Blanchett (not to mention how well GDT makes use of Buffalo's art deco scenery). In Frankenstein, I find the monster kind of boring and one-note once he starts talking and the movie becomes more faithful to the book. But scenes like the opening action sequence and Victor's demonstration with the partial body are where Del Toro really cuts loose and has a good time while also engaging with more complexity to the characters than the second half, which gets really moralistic.

The fact that we can have these opposing opinions I think shows how strong GDT's work really is, even if both halves don't work for everybody.
  - I thought his Nightmare Alley was a lesser film that the original version tbh. I genuinely have no clue how he managed to stretch out a 110 minute movie into a 150 minute one without adding a single plot point. Yes the ending in the original is studio happy ending garbage, but otherwise I thought the pace and tone were more enjoyable in the 40s version.
5. I don’t think of his recent output as harshly as you seem to, but I mostly agree. I liked, not loved, Frankenstein fwiw.

He’s almost becoming a manufactured parody of himself. “Del Toro” aesthetics with “oh yeah another girl falling for monster” trope. The plastic-y goth look with fantastical elements. Monsters are misunderstood, the freaks shall inherit the earth etc etc.

I don’t think it’s a bad as Burton, at least not yet. Burton seemed like he just gave up and went through the motions decades ago while Del Toro seems like he still cares. I just think Del Toro should do something to challenge himself and stay away from the obvious projects for himself. Anything with any similarity to Shape of Water should be avoided, but that seems to be what he wants the most.

Also on a side note, the “horror” elements of Frankenstein are overstated and if you read the book they are barely there. So I don’t hold the lack of horror against the film, I think that’s mostly normies who think it’s a “monster movie.” I do agree with other criticisms against it.
  - He made two movies right before Frankenstein that had nothing to do with those tropes (and his Frankenstein has one scene of "girl falling for a monster"). The Hellboy movies and Shape of Water are really it on that front. Really a huge stretch to say that a director finally getting to make a movie of one of his foundational influential texts, one which he has been trying to get made for 20 years, is becoming a manufactured parody of himself.
6. >Oddly digital look for period/gothic subject matter - Over lit

seeing this a lot, any camera people that can speak on this? I thought the single point lighting and color saturation were great but people are really hung up on this "too clean, digital" look, is it a product of like using a huge camera sensor and ISO that gives a very sharp image without imperfections?

## 2. [Crosspost] Hi reddit! I'm Edgar Wright. I've directed Shaun of the Dead, Hot Fuzz, Scott Pilgrim vs. the World, The World's End, Baby Driver, Last Night in Soho. The Running Man is out in theatres this FRIDAY. Ask me anything.

**Score:** 22
**Body:** I organized an AMA/Q&A with Edgar Wright, director/writer of films like Shaun of the Dead, Hot Fuzz, Scott Pilgrim vs. the World, The World's End, Baby Driver, Last Night in Soho, Sparks, and more! 

It's live here now in /r/movies for anyone that wants to ask questions:

https://www.reddit.com/r/movies/comments/1ouaau8/hi_reddit_im_edgar_wright_ive_directed_shaun_of/

He'll be back at 7:15 PM ET today (Tuesday 11/11) to answer questions. I recommend asking in advance. Please ask there, not here. All questions are much appreciated!

His newest film, The Running Man, is out in theaters worldwide this Friday. It stars Glen Powell, Josh Brolin, Colman Domingo, William H. Macy, Lee Pace, Michael Cera, Emilia Jones, Katy O'Brian, Daniel Ezra, and Karl Glusman.

Trailer:

https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=KD18ddeFuyM

Synopsis:

In the near future, "The Running Man" is the top-rated show on television, a deadly competition where contestants must survive 30 days while being hunted by professional assassins. Desperate for money to save his sick daughter, Ben Richards is convinced by the show's ruthless producer to enter the game as a last resort. Ratings soon skyrocket as Ben's defiance, instincts and grit turn him into an unexpected fan favorite, as well as a threat to the entire system.

His verification photo:

https://i.imgur.com/eX4zOQG.jpeg

### Top 0 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)


## 3. If "acquired taste" is a thing, how can one acquire the taste to enjoy arthouse, or simply more artistic cinema in general?

**Score:** 21
**Body:** This thread will need a bit of context behind it: Historically, I've watched very few movies, not even the most famous or mainstream ones. I had no reason for not doing it, I just didn't.  
Around two to three years ago, however, I was seeing an increasing number of people talking about the medium (Also the fact that in general I do enjoy video-essays about media, and references to cinema are frequent in them), and that got me curious: I would hear frequently of names such as "Felini", "Tarkovsky", "Griffith", "Bergman", or that "The Color of Pomegranate" was the most beautiful thing ever created. I would hear the names of a myriad different types of camera shots, I would hear terms such as "decoupage", "mise-en-scene", "composition" or "photography" without fully knowing what they were.

That all allured me to it, but having put years watching, studying and learning about another medium myself, I knew that jumping right into what they were talking outright wouldn't be a good idea, I likely wouldn't "get it" or know how to appreciate it's qualities (A few years earlier I had watched Fincher's "Se7en" and thought of it as just a normal movie, a reaction that seemed to displease a couple people), as I was a novice to cinema in general. So, at first, I decided to a good halfway point would be starting with works considered "arthouse"/"avant-garde"/"experimental" in the medium I had already been acquainted for more than a decade, anime.  
I had... mixed results (For what something that was supposed to be step one to already end in failure...).

A bit more than a year after having started the experiment, I went to discuss in a forum about one of such titles, Oshii Mamoru's 1985 "Tenshi no Tamago" in order to try to understand the appeal of it from the perspective of those who admired the work. In such thread, one of the answers (The most thorough, I must say) I received was the following:

>It's ok if you didn't enjoy it.  
Art has a milion forms and nobody has to like everything, growing up means accepting your tastes and preferences instead of molding them according tonwhat other people would like you to be.  
Some only like anime for the cool fights, some for the slow burners, some for the strange visuals etc  
Tenshi no Tamago is closer to a Jodorowsky or Tarkovsky movie, where plot and dialogue may be sparse or cryptic, but the focus is on the images and the use of the visual medium as a tool to convey messages, if you like movies for a structurally formalized narrative you won't like them, and that's okay, some people like the abstract presentation because it tickles the imagination and lets you interpret rather than accept what the story might be about.

What left me a bit uncomfortable about it was this seemingly fatalist perspective of "sometimes you simply can't like something, and nothing can be done about it", and this wasn't the first time in my life I had read something among such lines.  
It surprised the most, though, because it seemed to go against the very own term "acquired taste": Isn't it a taste you can learn how to have rather than simply having it from the start?

I wish to have this more intimate relationship with art, "being able to enjoy arthouse" being perhaps one of the final objectives (Or, at the very least, even if I'm not able to personally enjoy it, I could at least learn to appreciate what other people see in it). Currently, I'm unsure if I could even tell if something had good or bad directing, good or bad photography, good or bad composition, etc.

Is "loving arthouse" learnable? Is the taste, indeed, acquirable?

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Acquiring a taste means that the things that initially put you off might open back up when you persist and find entryways that do help. For instance, I hated *Last Year at Marienbad* when I first saw it but a decade on, having understood much more about several unrelated ideas and artistic perspectives, it became pleasurable.  
  
Watching film requires a constant process of negotiating your own ideas of taste and enjoyment and trying to surrender to the moment rather than wishing it was doing something else. But it's not easy and it doesn't happen overnight!

If you've watched 'very few' films, I'd suggest just...watching more. Mainstream, arthouse, independent, whatever. Just build up the reps and decide what aesthetics and eras please you most.
  - Watching more and reading more tbh. It’s important to expand your tastes generally, but I feel like reading is especially key for exposure to new ideas and their development, and that helps you to recognize them when they’re being explored in a film you’re watching.
2. Acquired taste to me is accessed through knowledge. Having a foundational knowledge of universally “great” films (top 500 before you die kind of thing), popular ones (main blockbusters like Jurassic Park, the Matrix etc) and a base level understanding at least of how movies are made and what makes the praised ones praised by so many. Then what those films influenced, follow the directors of the films you liked career work, look at who they’ve influenced since, and dig into their influences. Before you know it from that more generalized access point you develop your own personal preferences as you dig layer by layer into more art house cinema, experimental works etc.

A personal story of my own journey. I was a kid in the 90s, very much a product of the 90s. I grew up watching movies with my family so I already had an appreciation for them but it wasn’t until Pulp Fiction came out that my mind was literally blown away about the possibility of film. Up until that point movies were always just well movies. Pulp Fiction actually fundamentally shifted the way in which I viewed them. Now this is pre-Internet largely for the average home so back then we relied on books. I read and watched anything Tarantino had to do with. I had a copy of Reservoir Dogs I wore into the ground. I read the scripts he wrote, every script he had written to that point. That also gave me an understanding of how films were made in the first place. In the 90s Tarantino was really THE it director and he had this distribution line “Tarantino presents” where he would get the distro rights in the states for movies he loved. This was my entry to Wong Kar Wai by way of Chunking Express. Tarantino being my entry point was fortuitous because he is such an encyclopedia of film and his films themselves (much akin to sampling in hip hop) were essentially sampling of a bunch of his influences. Reservoir Dogs led me to A City on Fire led me to John Woo with The Killer and Hard Boiled etc. Following the threads backwards. The dialogue from Pulp Fiction and Tarantino scripts in general led me to Breathless which led me to the French new wave etc. My point of this story is find your access point and peel back from there.

It’s called “acquired taste” acquiring it comes from exposure, knowledge and understanding. Acquiring it takes time. So start with a wide net find your access point and start digging.
3. My recommendation is to stop watching anime for awhile.  It took minutes looking at your post history to see that you seem to have a borderline unhealthy relationship with that medium.  This is no offense intended towards you, or anime in general, but this seems to be a reoccurring theme that I have noticed quite a bit with friends, colleagues, relatives etc.  Just take a break from anime.  Stop trying to live "otaku" or whatever, go do something else for a week and reset yourself.  Then maybe start with a popular non-anime movie suggestion that gravitates towards you.
  - This quote from OP's history made me chuckle:

> Paul Thomas Anderson (IDK who he is, but he seems important)
4. One thing you could do is watch enough movies in general to find out what kind of movies you enjoy: genres, subjects, maybe actors, then seek out the arthouse versions of those kind of movies. There’s a lot of arthouse horror, crime and scifi films for example.
5. There are of course a ton of things you could do, like simply dry ”studying” to better understand the art form, but if you don’t already gravitate towards that idea it’s probably going to be boring etc.

The more frictionless and fun way of doing it, if you enjoy watching movies, is to simply make an extra effort to pick the more complex and ambitious films you already like or think you will like, and the films that seem adjacent to them. 

Simply watching more challenging films is going  to help you understand the medium better, especially if you try to understand what the filmmakers are trying to say or accomplish as you watch the films, and even spend some time after the film is over to understand it and appreciate it better over time.

Some good entry level filmmakers who’s films are very easy to enjoy but still make really great work with a lot to chew on are for example:

Stanley Kubrick

Martin Scorsese

Quentin Tarantino

Christopher Nolan 

David Fincher

Steven Spielberg 

Francis Ford Coppola 

Ridley Scott

Hayao Miyazaki 

Denis Villeneuve

Park Chan Wook 

Sergio Leone

David Lean

Michael Mann

Paul Thomas Anderson

Robert Eggers

Damien Chazelle 

The Coen brothers

Clint Eastwood

The Washowskis

Wes Anderson

Richard Linklater 

I think Stanley Kubrick is a particularly good choice because even though his films are really easy to enjoy, they are also very dense and you have to do a lot of the heavy lifting yourself. It also helps that he is one of the absolute best to ever make films.
6. Getting into cinema through anime is kind of wild, ngl.  
It’s like deciding to get into literature by starting with Marvel comics. Sure, there are a handful of genuinely great ones, but the medium as a whole isn’t built around what cinema’s best at. There are maybe a dozen or so anime films that stand shoulder-to-shoulder with great live-action cinema, and the rest tend to prioritize different things entirely.

But I think the bigger point is this: if a medium or style doesn’t do much for you, that’s fine. Even when I barely knew anything about movies, I was still moved by them; "It’s a Wonderful Life" made me cry, and "Dog Day Afternoon" completely gripped me when I was twelve. You don’t need a film education to be affected by great filmmaking.

Your story reminds me of when I tried to “get into” paintings because my friends were obsessed with them. I even read Gombrich’s "The Story of Art", which helped me understand them intellectually, but none ever made me *feel* anything. Sometimes appreciation can be learned, but emotional resonance can’t be forced.

  
EDIT: come to think of it, psilocybin actually made me resonate with paintings, later in life. Still though, it's an ability that is only present in me while under the influence of a substance.
7. Good question. For me, I think the answer is that sometimes you have to just watch movies knowing you won’t “get” them or understand why they are so beloved because they are a bit too artsy for your perspective if you haven’t watched many artsy movies.

Focus on finding elements within them that you appreciate even if the sum-total of the entire thing eludes you.
8. To counter all the male directors being bandied about, here are some women directors as well:

Claire Denis (*White Material* or *Beau Travail*)

Lynne Ramsay (*Ratcatcher*, *We Need to Talk About Kevin*)

Sophia Coppola (*The Virgin Suicides*)

Julia Ducornau (*Titane*)

Kelly Reichardt (*River of Grass*, *Certain Women*)

Miranda July (*Me and You and Everyone We Know*, *Kajillionaire*)


EDIT: good luck. I fell in love with arthouse immediately but my brain is already very associative so imagery and camera work made immediate sense to me as a teen just like my brain enjoys DeLillo more than say Patterson. Let go of the concrete and let association take rein.

## 4. A note on the ending of Manchester by the Sea

**Score:** 8
**Body:** Watched this for the first time last night. Fantastic.

I'd like to point out what I feel is a common misinterpretation about the ending, when Lee says "I can't beat it".

Many seem to see this as a moment of clarity, where Lee accepts that he will never move past this mistake and has to find a way forward despite that. While it is undeniable that nobody would be able to fully accept or escape such a tragic event, the movie makes it clear that it is Lee's way of coping with it that perpetuates his depressive state, and this final proclamation is just another manifestation of that perspective. In the context of Lee's own mindset, he finds some clarity and a path forward—a way to get away from Manchester while not stepping on Patrick's toes, and letting him live the life he wants to live.

His ex-wife Randi is basically a best-case-scenario example, given the circumstances. She has found something new in her life that makes her happy. And in this healed state, she offers the opportunity for something similar to Lee, as an alternative to his obvious deterioration. He rejects it. He can't handle his own emotions in the moment, the prospect of an extended conversation over lunch, or of truly coming to terms with what he did. I see his brother assigning Lee as the caretaker as him understanding what Lee needs—to face his past, process his emotions rather than deny them.

Lee finds a way out of this, and back into the state he's been in all these years. "I can't beat it" is a flawed statement—you're not supposed to "beat" it, at least in the sense of rising above it. You must first let it beat you down even if it means your messy emotions will get all over others. This of course would be mutually exclusive with the pride that motivates Lee's state—which does not allow him to be an emotional burden to others (yet he was almost willing to make Patrick abandon his life in Manchester), or to acknowledge himself as a victim of this tragedy like he does as the person responsible for it. This is not a moment of clarity, but a veiled perpetuation of Lee's sorry state, and a testament to how devastation and depression locks you into itself.

### Top 1 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I have an affinity with this film and watch it often. I’ve suffered some heavy tragedy, and I use this as a coping mechanism. I went through a similar weight loss as Lee, so that I may be taking this too close to heart. 

To me, that moment isn’t about defeat; it is honesty. You learn to live with it. That’s not the same as being trapped; it’s a quiet kind of survival

He’s done pretending that closure looks like everyone else’s version of healing. Randi rebuilt her life, and that’s beautiful, but Lee’s strength comes from something different: living every day while carrying what he can’t erase. 

‘I can’t beat it’ isn’t hopeless. It’s what happens when you stop trying to erase the worst thing that ever happened to you and start learning how to coexist with it—for some people, simply staying alive and functional after such a loss is a victory. I saw it more as a declaration to others, as I am living with this, I don’t want to be here, but I am.

## 5. Moonlight (2016) and the duality of Man

**Score:** 6
**Body:** I watched Moonlight for the 2nd time since seeing it around 2017ish. I picked up on several themes that I had not when i watched it the first time. A lot of the accolades came from the portrayal and discussion of Black male sexuality, which is understandable but I also saw another prominent theme in the movie: Man's (literally) duality.
The main character, Chiron is found by a local drug dealer (Juan) in an abandoned apartment that drug addicts use to do drugs after running away from boys who tease and harrass him for being gay (though throughout the movie, Chiron never defines his sexuality and has it thrust on him). Juan convinces Chiron to come out and buys him a meal. From there, they form a bond and Chiron sees him as a male role model to whom he turns when his mother is high and he doesn't want to/can't go home. A conversation with Juan is where Chrion's sexuality is discussed. One would assume Juan would be someone to tell him that he's not gay, he only likes women and it's not right to like boys. However, when Chiron asks "Am I a f*got?", Juan responds with "No, you're not a fgot. You can be gay, but don't let anyone call you a fg*ot." This is an unexpectedly open-minded response from someone living the life Juan lives. In this moment, it shows he accepts Chiron for who he is, no matter what. He just doesn't want the young boy to accept disrespect.
Another example is Kevin. Kevin is a classmate of Chrions when he's in high school. We meet Kevin after he gets caught having sex with a girl in the stairwell at school. He regales this to Chiron. Later, the 2 boys end up on the same beach one night and Chrion has his first(and only) sexual encounter. Kevin never divulges if he's interested in men-even at the end of the movie when we find out that he had a kid with a very attractive girl from high school. He doesn't show disgust when him and Chiron are sexual or when Chiron admits he was the only one to ever touch him. Kevin was much more popular than Chiron and obviously was sexual with women. Most (including the watcher) would not have expected that from Kevin (though this does tie into the sexuality theme too).
Finally, Chiron. Most of the movie, he is a shy, overly anxious boy of few words. He has his sexuality thrust on him, he doesn't have friends and he's very unconfident. However, through it all, most of us would not have expected him to become a drug dealer. The end of the movie shows us his life as an adult. He sells drugs in Atlanta, has slugs (gold "grillz") and is a significantly more dominating presence than in his younger years as a scrawny boy. When they meet, Kevin says that "that's not the Chiron I knew" to which Chiron asks him how well he truly knew him. Statistically, Chiron being around drugs and in the environment he was in made him more likely to use or sell them, but the boy we watched grow up was too timid to be into drug dealing. The adult Chiron isn't-though he is still a man of few words.
I'd like to think that these are great examples of the duality of man-that we have 2+ sides of us that don't necessarily seem like they should coexist. I'm just rambling now but I hope this gets the point across.
